1: The fourth year of Jehoiakim (605/604 BC) — the year Nebuchadnezzar defeated Egypt at Carchemish and became master of the Near East. It is also the year of Daniel's first deportation (Daniel 1:1). The convergence of dates links Jeremiah's great cup-of-wrath oracle with the beginning of Daniel's prophetic career in Babylon — the two prophets whose work together forms the chronological skeleton of the exile. Christ as the fulfiller of both prophecy streams enters history precisely at the end of their seventy-year and seventy-weeks countdowns (Daniel 9:24-27; Luke 3:23).
2: Jeremiah as the prophet who speaks to all Judah — the comprehensive audience of the oracle. Jesus likewise addresses all Jerusalem in his final temple discourses (Matt 23-25), culminating in the same predictions of catastrophe. The prophet who must speak to the whole people even when they do not listen is the prototype of Christ, the final prophet (Deuteronomy 18:15-18; Hebrews 1:1-2), whose words go out to all people even when many reject them.
3: "Twenty-three years the word of the LORD has come to me and I have spoken." The long faithfulness of the prophet — speaking without visible fruit for two decades. Hebrews 11:36-38 describes those who suffered through faithfulness, "of whom the world was not worthy." Christ's own ministry of three and a half years, in which the majority rejected him, inherits and concentrates this pattern of faithful speaking to the unhearing. His ministry was not measured by acceptance but by obedience to the one who sent him (John 12:49).
4: The persistent-sending formula: "the LORD has persistently sent you all his servants the prophets." Hashkem v'shaleach — rising early and sending — is Jeremiah's signature phrase for YHWH's persistent patience. Hebrews 1:1-2 is the direct theological commentary on this verse: "In the past God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets at many times and in various ways, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son." Christ is the Son sent as the final, definitive word after all the prophetic sendings have been rejected.
5: "Turn back from your evil way and live in the land." The covenant call to repentance with the promise of remaining in the land. Jesus's first proclamation (Mark 1:15 — "repent and believe the good news") is structurally identical to this: the call to turn back, now with the kingdom of God as what the turning-back enables inhabiting. Where the land was the covenant blessing in Jeremiah, in Christ the "land" expands to encompass the new creation that repentance opens onto (Romans 4:13 — Abraham and his offspring inherit the world).
6: "Do not follow other gods... and do not provoke me to anger." The prohibition against idolatry as the center of the covenant. Paul's treatment of idolatry in Romans 1:18-25 and 1 Corinthians 10:14-22 is the NT explication of this warning. Christ enters this framework as the one who perfectly did not follow other gods — the temptation narratives (Matthew 4:8-10 — "worship the Lord your God and serve him only") show him refusing the idolatrous shortcut and maintaining exclusive covenant loyalty throughout.
7: "You have not listened to me and have provoked me to anger with what your hands have made." The verdict of covenant failure — the idols of human manufacture versus the God who made human hands. Acts 17:24-25 (Paul at Athens): "the God who made the world and everything in it... is not served by human hands, as if he needed anything." Christ is YHWH in human hands making not idols but the healing and reconciliation that human hands could never achieve on their own (John 1:14 — the Word made flesh).
8: "Because you have not obeyed my words." The therefore-clause that introduces the judgment. Romans 5:19 — "through the disobedience of the one man the many were made sinners." But the second half of the verse is the gospel: "through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous." The judgment that falls because of disobedience (Jeremiah 25:8) is met by the obedience of Christ that creates a new righteousness for all who are in him. The "because you have not obeyed" is answered by his "because he obeyed."
9: "I am going to summon all the peoples of the north and Nebuchadnezzar my servant." The designation of a pagan king as YHWH's servant is theologically striking — it asserts divine sovereignty over all nations, not just Israel. Romans 13:1 ("there is no authority except that which God has established") and Revelation 17:17 ("God has put it into their hearts to accomplish his purpose") extend this principle. Christ is the ultimate servant (ebed / doulos) in whom this pattern culminates — not a pagan instrument but the willing, fully obedient Son who gives himself (Philippians 2:7-8).
10: "I will silence among them the sounds of joy and gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the bride, the grinding of millstones." The silencing of ordinary life — joy, marriage, daily work. Revelation 18:22-23 quotes this verse almost verbatim in the lament over Babylon's fall: "the sound of harpists and musicians... will never be heard in you again... the voice of the bridegroom and bride will never be heard in you again." What Jeremiah pronounces against Jerusalem, Revelation declares against Babylon — but both are reversed by Christ's own wedding feast (Revelation 19:7-9) where the bridegroom's voice is heard forever.
11: "These nations will serve the king of Babylon for seventy years." The seventy-year exile-clock that Daniel reads as he seeks YHWH's face (Daniel 9:2). Daniel's prayer (Daniel 9:3-19) generates the angel's response: seventy weeks are decreed for your people — the prophecy of the 490 years that culminates in the anointed one being cut off (Daniel 9:26). Christ arrives at the exact terminus of Daniel's recalculation of Jeremiah's seventy years. Jeremiah 25:11 sets the clock; Christ is its appointed hour.
12: "When seventy years are over, I will punish the king of Babylon." The accountability of the instrument of judgment — even YHWH's tools are judged for how they carry out their task. Revelation 18 is the extended fulfillment of the judgment on Babylon that Jeremiah 25:12 and chapters 50-51 announce. Christ presides over this final judgment: Revelation 19:11-16 — the rider on the white horse whose name is "the Word of God" executes the judgment that Jeremiah prophesied and Revelation completes.
13: "All that is recorded in this book, which Jeremiah prophesied against all the nations." The written prophecy as the permanent record against which history is measured. 2 Peter 3:7 ("the present heavens and earth are reserved for fire, kept for the day of judgment") and Revelation 20:12 ("the books were opened") place Christ at the center of the final accounting against which all written prophecy finds its vindication. The scroll of Jeremiah 25:13 becomes the opened books of the final assize that Christ presides over.
14: "Many nations and great kings will enslave them in turn, and I will repay them." The reciprocity of divine justice — what Babylon did to others will be done to Babylon. Galatians 6:7 — "God cannot be mocked. A man reaps what he sows." Christ himself teaches this in the parable of the unmerciful servant (Matthew 18:21-35) — the one who was forgiven much and forgave nothing receives the same treatment they meted out. Divine repayment is not vindictiveness but the moral coherence of the universe Christ died to restore.
15: "Take this cup of the wine of wrath from my hand, and make all the nations to whom I send you drink it." The cup of divine wrath — the most direct OT antecedent to Gethsemane. Matthew 26:39 — "My Father, if it is possible, may this cup be taken from me." Mark 14:36 — "Father, everything is possible for you. Take this cup from me. Yet not what I will, but what you will." The cup that Jeremiah is commanded to carry to all nations is the cup Christ takes into himself. Isaiah 51:17-23 promises that the cup will pass from Zion; it passes to Christ, who drinks it to the dregs (John 18:11 — "Shall I not drink the cup the Father has given me?").
16: "They will drink and reel and go out of their minds because of the sword I am sending among them." The effect of drinking the cup — disorientation and destruction. Christ drank the cup and in doing so absorbed its disorienting power: the cry of desolation (Matthew 27:46 — "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?") is the moment of maximum cup-drinking, the nadir of wrath absorbed. He experienced the reeling so that those who drink the new-covenant cup of his blood (1 Corinthians 11:25-26) receive not wrath but the Lord's death proclaimed until he comes.
17: "So I took the cup from the LORD's hand and made all the nations drink it." Jeremiah's obedience — the prophet who carries and presents the cup of judgment. Christ carries the cup one step further: not just to the nations but into himself. He is both the prophet who presents the cup (as Jeremiah) and the one who drinks it himself (as the Lamb). John 1:29 — "the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world" — the sin-bearing is the cup-drinking, now applied to all nations.
18: Jerusalem and the cities of Judah as the first to receive the cup. "It is time for judgment to begin with God's household" (1 Peter 4:17) — the same principle that YHWH begins with his own people. The cross occurred in Jerusalem — judgment began where the covenant was centered. Christ died in the city that first received the cup in Jeremiah's vision, bearing the judgment that the city had accumulated through centuries of covenant violation.
19: Pharaoh king of Egypt named among the cup-recipients. The exodus from Egypt that defined Israel's identity is now inverted: Egypt's king must drink alongside Judah's king. The great power that once held God's people captive now faces the same divine judgment as those it once enslaved. Christ's redemption creates a new exodus (Luke 9:31 — his "departure" / exodos is discussed at the transfiguration) that transcends the political boundaries of Jeremiah's cup-list.
20: The foreign peoples, the land of Uz (Job's homeland), Philistia — the cup extends to the nations at Israel's borders. Romans 3:9 — "both Jews and Gentiles alike are all under the power of sin." The cup-list of Jeremiah 25:19-26 is the OT prototype of Paul's universal indictment: all are under judgment and therefore all are eligible for the universal salvation Christ provides. The comprehensiveness of the cup-list matches the comprehensiveness of the gospel invitation.
21: Edom, Moab, and the Ammonites — the eastern neighbors, descendants of Esau and Lot. Numbers typically associated with Israel's historical enemies and occasional allies. The cup crosses kinship lines: these nations share Abraham's lineage in various ways (Edom from Esau; Moab and Ammon from Lot) but must still drink. The universal judgment that disregards ethnic connection anticipates the universal gospel that equally disregards it: Galatians 3:28 — "neither Jew nor Gentile... for you are all one in Christ Jesus."
22: Tyre and Sidon — the Phoenician trading cities whose prosperity was legendary. Jesus uses Tyre and Sidon as comparators in his judgment-speech: Matthew 11:21-22 — "if the miracles performed in you had been performed in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago." The cities that were spared fuller judgment in Jeremiah's vision become in Jesus's discourse the benchmark against which Capernaum's greater refusal is measured. Christ uses the cup-list nations to calibrate the severity of rejecting him.
23: Dedan, Tema, Buz — the Arabian trade-route peoples. The cup extends into the heart of the Arabian desert. Isaiah 21:13-17 also addresses the Arabian peoples in oracles of judgment. The geographic completeness of the cup-list (from Egypt to Media to Arabia) reflects the universal scope of divine sovereignty. Revelation 14:6-7 — "an angel flying in midair, with the eternal gospel to proclaim to those who live on the earth — to every nation, tribe, language, and people" — the proclamation to all has the same geographic comprehensiveness as Jeremiah's cup-list.
24: The kings of Arabia and "the various peoples who live in the desert." No human sovereignty escapes the cup — even the nomadic desert peoples who seem beyond the reach of settled kingdoms are included. Revelation 7:9 — "a great multitude that no one could count, from every nation, tribe, people, and language" — the peoples drawn from the desert and the margins in Jeremiah's cup-judgment appear in Revelation's vision as the redeemed from every nation standing before the throne.
25: Elam and Media — the eastern empires that would later overthrow Babylon. They too drink the cup they will later administer to others. The irony is theological: even the destroyers of Babylon are themselves under divine judgment. No instrument of YHWH's justice escapes the scrutiny that it applies to others. Romans 2:1 — "you who pass judgment on someone else are condemning yourself, because you who pass judgment do the same things." Christ alone judges without being himself under judgment (John 8:46 — "Can any of you prove me guilty of sin?").
26: "All the kingdoms on the face of the earth — and last of all, the king of Sheshak will drink it." Sheshak is the atbash cipher for Babylon (š-š-k = b-b-l reversed) — the cryptographic naming of the empire that controls the world. Revelation 17-18 takes up this Babylon-mystery with full elaboration: "Babylon the Great, the mother of prostitutes... the great city that rules over the kings of the earth." Christ's judgment of Babylon (Revelation 19:2 — "he has condemned the great prostitute") is the fulfillment of the Sheshak cup-judgment Jeremiah encodes.
27: "Drink, get drunk and vomit; fall down and never rise again." The comprehensive devastation of those who drink the cup of wrath to the end. At Gethsemane Christ prays "let this cup pass from me" (Matthew 26:39) — this is the cup he is asking about, the cup that causes falling-and-not-rising. He does not refuse it. He drinks it fully on the cross. Isaiah 51:22-23 promises that "never again will you drink it" — the cup that made Zion fall has passed to another. The one who drank it rose: the resurrection is the reversal of the cup's falling-and-not-rising.
28: "If they refuse to take the cup from your hand to drink, say to them: You must certainly drink." The divine insistence on universal accountability — there is no opt-out from divine judgment. 2 Corinthians 5:10 — "we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ, so that each of us may receive what is due us for the things done while in the body." Those who refuse the cup-of-judgment from Jeremiah's hand have not escaped it — they face the same Christ at the judgment seat that Jeremiah's oracle announces for all nations.
29: "I am already beginning to bring disaster on the city that bears my own name — do you think you will escape judgment? You will not go unpunished." The logic of Jeremiah's universalism: if YHWH judges even the city that carries his name, no one escapes on the grounds of proximity to him. Romans 2:9-11 — "there will be trouble and distress for every human being who does evil — first for the Jew, then for the Gentile... for God does not show favoritism." Christ does not grant exemption to the covenant insiders; his judgment at the temple ("not one stone will be left on another") is the enacted form of Jeremiah 25:29.
30: "The LORD will roar from on high; from his holy dwelling he will thunder." The divine roar from Zion — echoed in Joel 3:16 and Amos 1:2. Revelation 5:5 — "do not weep! See, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has triumphed." Christ is simultaneously the Lion who roars in judgment (Jeremiah 25:30; Amos 1:2) and the Lamb who was slain (Revelation 5:6). The two images exist in perfect tension in Revelation's throne-room vision: the roaring-lion power of Jeremiah 25:30 is expressed through the slaughtered lamb's sacrifice. The shout of judgment is also the shout of the winepress-treader (Revelation 19:15).
31: "He is entering into judgment with all flesh; the wicked he will put to the sword." The global judgment: kol-bāśār — all flesh. John 5:22-27 — "the Father judges no one, but has entrusted all judgment to the Son... for he is the Son of Man." The judgment of all flesh that YHWH announces in Jeremiah 25:31 is given to Christ. Acts 17:31 — "he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed." The "all flesh" scope of Jeremiah's oracle becomes the "all people" scope of the final judgment with Christ as judge.
32: "Disaster is spreading from nation to nation, and a great storm is rising from the far ends of the earth." The global reach of divine judgment — no geography is exempt. Matthew 24:14 — "this gospel of the kingdom will be preached in the whole world as a testimony to all nations, and then the end will come." The storm-from-the-ends-of-the-earth that Jeremiah announces as judgment and the gospel-to-the-ends-of-the-earth that Jesus announces as salvation share the same geographical comprehensiveness. Both are unstoppable and universal; the difference is what they carry.
33: "Those killed by the LORD on that day will stretch from one end of the earth to the other. No one will mourn them, no one will gather them, no one will bury them." The eschatological slaughter — the scale defying normal grief-practices. Revelation 19:17-21 depicts the same scene as the battle of Armageddon: "an angel standing in the sun who cried in a loud voice to all the birds flying in midair, 'Come, gather together for the great supper of God.'" Christ is the presider over this final day, not its victim. He who was left unburied (no permanent tomb — he rose) presides over the final accounting of the unburied dead.
34: "Wail, you shepherds, and cry out! Roll in the dust, you leaders of the flock! For the time of your slaughter has arrived." The shepherds who destroyed rather than tended must now face the flock's fate themselves. Ezekiel 34 is the extended meditation on this — the bad shepherds who scatter the flock. Christ's answer is John 10:11 — "I am the good shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep." He does the opposite of the wailing shepherds: they flee from their own slaughter; he stays and accepts his. The shepherd-judgment of Jeremiah 25:34 is the dark background against which the good-shepherd christology of John 10 shines.
35: "The shepherds will have nowhere to flee, and the leaders of the flock no way to escape." The inescapability of judgment for those who abused their leadership. Hebrews 2:3 — "how shall we escape if we ignore so great a salvation?" The question inverts Jeremiah's judgment-logic: it is not "how will the wicked shepherds escape?" but "how will those who ignore Christ's salvation escape?" Christ himself has no flight from the cross (Mark 14:41-42 — "the hour has come; the Son of Man is delivered into the hands of sinners. Rise! Let us go!") — he stays while the disciples flee.
36: "The cry of the shepherds, the wailing of the leaders of the flock — for the LORD is devastating their pasture." The reversal of pastoral blessing: the pasture that the good shepherd tends has been ravaged by the bad shepherd's negligence. John 10:10 — "the thief comes only to steal and kill and destroy; I came that they may have life and have it abundantly." The devastated pasture of Jeremiah 25:36 is the aftermath of the hireling-shepherd's failure; Christ's "abundant life" is the restoration of the pasture to its intended fruitfulness.
37: "The peaceful grazing lands are laid waste because of the LORD's fierce anger." The nāwôt haššālôm — the peaceable pastures — destroyed. Psalms 23:2 — "he makes me lie down in green pastures." The good shepherd restores what the fierce-anger judgment destroys. Christ as the shepherd who leads to still waters (Ps 23) and the shepherd-king of Ezekiel 34:23-24 ("I will place over them one shepherd, my servant David") is the answer to the devastated-pasture oracle of Jeremiah 25:37. The peaceable pastures are restored in the eschatological shepherd-reign of the Son of David.
38: "Like a lion he has left his den, for their land has become a desolation." YHWH as the lion who has left — the protective divine presence withdrawn, the land left to desolation. Christ's coming is the return of the protective presence: Matthew 28:20 — "surely I am with you always, to the very end of the age." The lion who left the den in judgment comes back as the Lion of Judah (Revelation 5:5) who establishes his permanent presence in the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:3 — "God's dwelling place is now among the people"). The divine absence of Jeremiah 25:38 is answered by the permanent divine presence of Christ's new covenant.
